Gout Gout is a recurrent acute arthritis of peripheral joints which results from deposition, in and about the joints and tendons, of crystals of monosodium urate from supersaturated hyperuricaemic body fluids. The arthritis may become chronic. Clinical presentation • The main clinical features are those of an acute gouty arthritis, often nocturnal, throbbing crushing or excruciating pain. • The signs resemble an acute infection with swelling, hot red and very tender joints. • The first metatarsophalangeal joint of the big toe is frequently involved Investigation • Serum uric acid level. Non-Pharmacological Treatment • In obese patient, reduce weight • Avoid

ender joints. • The first metatarsophalangeal joint of the big toe is frequently involved Investigation • Serum uric acid level. Nutritional support To prevent excessive accumulation of uric acid • Use of low purine diet by restricting consumption red meat, fish, alcohol, stimulants, and high protein foods to avoid exogenous addition of purines to the existing high uric acid load is recommended • Encourage consumption of alkalizing foods e.g. lemons, tomatoes, green beans, fruits and milk products • Intake of fluids about 3lts/day to enhance excretion of uric acid based on assessment is recommended • Moderate protein intake (0.8g/kg/day) • Maintain adequate CHO intake to prevent ketosis • Limit fat intake • Avoid large and heavy meals late in the evening • Encourage consumption of whole grains 17.2.4 Osteoarthritis It is a common form of arthritis, characterized by degenerative loss of articular cartilage, subchondral bony sclerosis, and cartilage and bone proliferation subsequent osteophyte formation. Gradual onset of one or a few joints involved. 17.2.5 Heterotopic Ossification Heterotopic ossification is defined as bone formation in no osseous tissues. Usually occurs in trauma such as fractures and surgical procedures, the exact cause remains unknown. Clinical presentation • Pain and reduced ROM following trauma or post-surgical intervention. • swelling and tenderness, which can mimic a low-grade infection. • Bone in soft tissue starts to appear on plain radiographs 4 to 6 weeks after the trauma/surgery, Pharmacological Treatment • Indomethacin 75- 150mg per day in single daily dose or divided dose 12hrly Surgical management Surgery is delayed for 6 months after the initial trauma/surgery to allow the bone to mature and a distinct fibrous capsule to develop. 17.2.6 Tendinopathies These are clinical syndrome characterized by chronic, localized tendon pain exacerbated by mechanical loading. Caused mostly by tendon overuse and metabolic factors. Classified as Acute or Chronic. Clinical presentation • Pain with palpation of the affected • Kinetic chain changes (muscle part weakness, abnormal movement • Pain during tendon loading which patterns or joint stiffness) may be also referred pain. • Muscle spasm Investigations • MRI for tendinopathy involving deep tendon like around the shoulder joint • Greyscale or color Doppler uss. Non-pharmacological Treatment • Physiotherapy (active exercise, stretching) • Activity modificati

or tendinopathy involving deep tendon like around the shoulder joint • Greyscale or color Doppler uss. Non-pharmacological Treatment • Physiotherapy (active exercise, stretching) • Activity modification • Correction of any biomechanical faults • Reduction of aggravating activities • Ice heat (chronic tendinopathy) • Joint mobilization and friction (massage). OR S: triamcinolone 40mg per injection site once weekly not to exceed 40mg/day maximum of 3 injections in one year at interval of 1-3 weeks between injections. Open surgery or laparoscopic 17.2.7 Plantar Fasciitis Planter Fasciitis is a degenerative disease of plantar fascia probably due to overuse trauma that leads to micro tears. Depending on duration of symptoms it can be acute 4-6 weeks, subacute 6-12 weeks and chronic >12weeks with/without refractory. Clinical presentation • Pain in the medial side of the heel, most noticeable with initial steps after a period of inactivity • Paresthesia • Tightness of Achilles tendon Investigations • Plain X-ray • Ultrasound • Nerve conduction and Electromyographic studies • MRI in chronic with refractory. Contrary to Brittle bone disease which are inheritable with many different phenotypic presentation, however medication for the treatment of these two disease are more or less similar. different phenotypic presentation, however medication for the treatment of these two disease are more or less similar. Clinical presentation • Presence of fragility fracture (spine, hip, wrist, humerus, rib and pelvis) • Family history of osteoporotic fracture • Bone pain Investigation • Complete blood count • Parathyroid stimulating hormone • Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry • Thyroid stimulating hormone (DEXA) to measure spine and hip • Serum creatinine bone density as reference point • Urea • Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D, • Serum electrolyte • Serum Calcium Diagnostic criteria T-score chart based on the mineral density measurement by DXA. Osteoporosis is diagnosed when a person’s BMD is equal to or more than 2.5 standard deviations below this reference measurement. Status Hip BMD Normal T-score of -1 or above Osteopenia T-score lower than -1 and greater than -2.5 Osteoporosis T-score of -2.5 or lower Severe osteoporosis T-score of -2.5 or lower, and presence of at least one fragility fracture 382

Amputation is the surgical removal of part of a limb or extremity such as an arm, leg, foot, hand, toe, or finger. This is the treatment of choice for diseased limbs and devastating extremity injuries for which attempts at salvage and reconstruction may be lengthy, emotionally, and financially costly and have a less than satisfactory results. Clinical Indication • Trauma most common reason for • Vascular disease extremity amputation • Metabolic diseases • Infection • Congenital anomalies • Tumor Goals of amputation • Preserve functional length. • Prevention of adjacent joint • Preservation of useful sensibility contractures • Preven

etabolic diseases • Infection • Congenital anomalies • Tumor Goals of amputation • Preserve functional length. • Prevention of adjacent joint • Preservation of useful sensibility contractures • Prevention of symptomatic • Early prosthetic fitting neuromas Early return of patient to work and recreation. 17.4.1: Diabetic Foot Ulcers A common surgical complication in diabetic patients. Ulcers can be secondarily infected by staphylococci, streptococci, coliforms, and anaerobic bacteria which can lead to cellulitis, abscess formation, gangrene, and osteomyelitis. Clinical presentation The three main factors that lead to tissue necrosis in the diabetic foot are: • Neuropathy • Infection • Ischaemia Non-pharmacological Treatment • Metabolic control • Relieve pressure: non-weight bearing 384

• Smoking cessation is essential. • Frequent (e.g. weekly) removal of • Wound care excess keratin and allow efficient drainage of the lesion. Pharmacological Treatment B: ceftriaxone (IV) 1-2g 24hourly for 7-10days AND B: metronidazole (IV) 500mg 8hourly for 7-10days Depending on culture results S: piperacillin-tazobactam (IV) 4.5g 6-8hourly 7-10days (for severe/complicated cases) OR S: meropenem (IV) 1g 8hourly 7-10days Surgical Management • Surgical debridement • Amputation depending Wagner’s classification Table 17:4: Wageners Classification for Diabetic Foot Ulcers ULCER GRADING DESCRIPTION GRADE 0 No ulcer but high-risk foot GRADE 1 Superficial ulcer GRADE 2 Deep ulcer, no bony involvement or abscess GRADE 3 Abscess with bony involvement (as shown by X- ray) GRADE 4 Localized gangrene e.g toe, heel etc GRADE 5 Extensive gangrene involving the whole foot Note Grade 1-3 ulcers are termed non-gangrenous ulcers and Grade 4 and 5 ulcers are termed gangrenous ulcers 17.5 Affection of the Brain and Spine 17.51 Low Back Pain Low back pain is a common presenting complaint especially among the adult population. Severity may range from mild, transient symptom to chronic and disabling pain. Causes of low back pain are numerous and clues to the underlying cause can usually be found from a good clinical history and physical examination. In some patients however, no cause will be found,

n. Causes of low back pain are numerous and clues to the underlying cause can usually be found from a good clinical history and physical examination. In some patients however, no cause will be found, and these people are described as having nonspecific back pain. Clinical presentation Exclude specific causes of low back pain, for example, suspected or confirmed malignancies, spine infection, spine trauma or inflammatory disease such as spondyloarthritis. (flag signs) Acute low back pain (pain for <6 weeks’ duration) Investigations • Explain to people with acute low back pain with or without sciatica that their pain is likely to resolve on analgesia and bed rest • Do not routinely offer imaging for people with acute low back pain with or without sciatica in absence of flag signs. Consider imaging only if the result is likely to change management. Non-pharmacological Treatment Self-management: Provide people with advice and information, tailored to their needs and capabilities, to help them self-manage their low back pain. • information on the nature of low back pain and sciatica • encouragement to continue with normal activities upon pain relief. 385

Physical Exercise: Encourage exercise programmes (biomechanical, aerobics, or a combination of approaches) based on individual needs, preferences, and capabilities. The surgical approach might entail an open or minimal invasive technique based on patient factors, availability of resources and expertise. 17.5.2: Cervical Degenerative Disorders Cervical degenerative spine disorders (DSD)are common among the adult population. Clinical characteristics include neck pain radiating to upper extremities related to compression and/or irritation of one or more cervical nerve roots, resulting into varying degrees of sensory, motor and reflex changes. Severity may range from mild, transient symptom to chronic and disabling pain. Clinical presentation • neck pain, dermatomal arm pain, scapular/periscapular pain • upper limb paresthesias, numbness and sensory changes, • muscle cramps, weakness, or abnormal deep tendon reflexes Investigations • Plain Spine x rays • Dynamic spine x-Rays-Flexion/extension views • Cervical Spine MRI • EMG can be considered for patients in whom the diagnosis is unclear after MRI Non-pharmacological Treatment Non Invasive treatment for neck pain and radiculopathies Physical Exercis

For radicular symptoms add neuromodulators, neurovitamins and muscle relaxants: Neuromodulators D: pregabalin 75-150mg 24hourly for 4weeks (dose can be escalated based on individual response) AND Neurovitamins C: vit B 1 +B 6 +B 12 (PO) 24hourly for 4weeks AND Muscle relaxant S: baclofen 5mg 8hourly initially, increase by 5mg/dose each after 3days up to 20 mg 8hourly for up to 2 weeks OR S: tizanidine initially 2mg 8hourly, increase gradually to 4mg per day every 1-4days, dose may be extended for 4weeks or more, to discontinue taper gradually; decrease by 2-4mg daily Calcium supplementation B: Calcium 600mg + Vitamin D800IU (PO) 24hourly for 3 months then re evaluate (1mcg of Vitamin D=40 international unit) Bisphosphonate treatment – should be given for a period of five years. S: ibandronate (IV) 3mg every 3months administered over 15-20minutes (treatment only) for 5years 17.6: Common Congenital Anomalies of Musculoskeletal System 17:6:1 Talipes equinovarus (congenital clubfoot) Talipes equinovarus is one of the most common musculoskeletal birth defect affecting approx. 1/1,000 live births. If untreated it causes long-term functional disability. The condition can vary in its degree of severity ranging from quite mild to very severe. It may present with other defects such as spina bifida, arthrogryposis, multiplex congenita, congenital myotonic dystrophy, or diastrophic dysplas

e of severity ranging from quite mild to very severe. It may present with other defects such as spina bifida, arthrogryposis, multiplex congenita, congenital myotonic dystrophy, or diastrophic dysplasia. Clinical presentation Birth deformities of the foot with • Small foot and calf • Varus (tight tendoachilles, tibialis • Cavus (tight intrinsics muscles) posterior, tibialis anterior) • Adductus of forefoot (tight tibialis • Equinus (tight tendoachilles) posterior) Diagnosis • Most clinical diagnosis • Plain X-ray Management Non operative management - Ponseti method of serial manipulation and casting, The goal is to rotate foot laterally around a fixed talus, order of correction follows acronym (CAVE) 1. Cavus 2. Adductus3.Varus4. Equinus. Heel cord tenotomy needed in at least 80-90% of children. Followed by Foot abduction orthosis (FAO). Non noncompliance to FAO is the biggest risk factor for deformity recurrence Table 17:5: Ponseti Method Month Weekly serial casting (with knee • First correct cavus with forefoot SUPINATED (NOT 1-4 in 90° of flexion ) with forefoot pronated) by aligning the less varus forefoot with the supination, then forefoot more varus hindfoot (pronation would increase cavus abduction deformity) • Secondly correct adduction and heel varus by rotating calcaneus and forefoot around talus (head of talus acts as a fulcrum) into forefoot ABDUCTION 394

Tendon Achilles lengthening • Equinus correction last with tendon Achilles (Tenotomy) (TAL) at week 8 tenotomy required in > 80-90% •Perform when foot is at least 60° abducted, heel is in valgus and equinus persists •Cast in maximal dorsiflexion for 3 weeks after tenotomy Month Foot abduction orthosis (FAO) •With FAO holding affected feet at least 60°external 4-8 •23 hours a day for 3 months rotation and 30° in normal foot for unilateral cases after correction •night time/nap time only until •Feet are measured prior to tenotomy so FAO is age 4 years available on the day of post-tenotomy cast removal 2-4 Tibialis anterior tendon transfer •30-50% will need TA transfer with or without repeat years (TA transfer) at 2-5 yrs of age TAL or gastrocnemius recession for recurrent (30-50% will require) deformity •Indicated if the patient demonstrates supination during gait Operative management I. posteromedial soft tissue release and tendon lengthening for children of <2years II. medial column lengthening or lateral column-shortening osteotomy, or cuboid decancellation indicated for children older than 2years III. talectomy-n severe, rigid recurrent clubfoot in children between 6-10yeras or those with arthrogryposis Note Surgical management are complemented by serial casting and foot adduction orthosis 17.7: Musculoskeletal Tumour Musculoskeletal tumors are tumors that develop in muscles,

hose with arthrogryposis Note Surgical management are complemented by serial casting and foot adduction orthosis 17.7: Musculoskeletal Tumour Musculoskeletal tumors are tumors that develop in muscles, bones and nerves Bone tumors are divided into primary and secondary (metastatic) tumours. Primary tumours arise from cells which constitute the bone and are divided into benign and malignant types. Metastatic type is most common cause of bone cancer, the most common primary sites being renal, thyroid, lung, prostate, and breast. Clinical presentation • Dull and aching pain which worsen at night is deemed a ‘red flag’ symptom • Swelling where the tumor is located • Pathological fracture • Fever, weight loss, anemia and general body malaise are late symptoms Note For detailed clinical presentations, investigations and management of specific bone tumor refer to malignancy chapter 17:8 Angular Deformity of Limbs 17.8.1 Rickets Refers to softening and weakening of bones in children, usually due to inadequate vitamin D. Vitamin D promotes the body’s absorption of calcium and phosphorus. Extreme or prolonged lack of vitamin D makes it difficult to maintain proper calcium and phosphorus levels in bones, which can cause rickets. Clinical presentation • delayed growth, • bow legs, • weakness and pain in the spine, • pelvis and leg 395

Pharmacological Treatment Treatment may involve adding vitamin D or calcium to the diet, 17.8.2 Physiological Bowing/Knocked Knees Is considered bending of knees at an early age due to early walking and overweight below the age of 2years Diagnosis process I. Diagnosis is mainly by physical examination – obvious varus/valgus deformity and metaphysical widening. II. Blood tests to measure the levels of calcium and phosphate in the blood (Refer to table 17:3.) III. Bone X-rays to check for bone deformities. Treatment • First consultation: rule out if it’s physiological – bow legs at young age before the age of 3 years without signs of rickets • If it’s physiological then consider follow up every 6 months +/- vitamin d supplements • At the age of 3 years if deformity more than 8degrees consider–hemiepiphysiodesis at around the age of 3years if the deformity is due to Blount’s disease consider hemiepiphysiodesis/corrective osteotomy. Note For details on specific surgical option kindly refer to standard orthopedic text books. 396

CHAPTER EIGHTEEN TRAUMA & INJURIES 18.1 General Management of Trauma and Injuries Trauma may occur as a result of motor vehicle crash, fights, falls, gunshots, sports, animal bites etc. and is associated with head, musculoskeletal, visceral, and neurovascular injuries. The aim of managing trauma is to prevent further injuries and life-threatening complications that may lead to increased morbidity and mortality. There is a systematic trauma protocol in place, ABCDE protocol, emphasizing on the primary survey, resuscitation, secondary survey, and finally definitive care. Clinical Presentation • There is a history of trauma • Level of consciousness • ABCDE General Treatment Community/Dispensary Level Interventions • Clear the airway, protect the cervical spine Minimize bleeding by applying direct pressure and dress wounds • Assess cardiac function: arterial pulse, BP and capillary refill. • Administer analgesics for pain control A: diclofenac (IM) 75mg 8hourly for 1-3days • Splint long bone fractures and immobilize the spine o Use available splints for long bone fractures o sandbags/ bedsheet /neck collar for cervical spine o Long rigid spinal board or immobilization on stretcher • If unconscious put in coma position and protect the spine. • Consider anti-tetanus prophylaxis according to the protocol (see table below) • Close open-book pelvic fractures using abdominal pelvic binde

unconscious put in coma position and protect the spine. • Consider anti-tetanus prophylaxis according to the protocol (see table below) • Close open-book pelvic fractures using abdominal pelvic binder/bed sheet • Refer to the centers/health facilities where there is expertise. Health Centre Level Interventions • Manage as above, capitalizing on ABCDE Trauma Protocol, • Review the resuscitation therapy given before arrival • Maintain on rigid cervical spine collar • Catheterize bladder in unconscious patient. • Give IV Normal Saline or Ringer’s Lactate • Do not feed patient, if unconscious put an NGT tube and an oxygen face mask • If there are open wounds do surgical debridement and dress: A: ampicillin (IV) 500mg 6hourly for 3days OR B: chloramphenicol (IV) 500mg 6hourly for 3days OR B: ampicillin + cloxacillin (IV) (FDC) 500mg 8hourly for 3days • Anti-tetanus prophylaxis according to the protocol (see table 18.1 below) • Give ant rabies according to the protocol where indicated • Administer oxygen tom maintain SPO2>92% • Refer if • Comatose for ICU care and brain CT scan • Open fracture if surgery not feasible • Failed to resuscitate, need blood transfusion or laparotomy is necessary Hospital Level Interventions • Manage as above-primary and secondary survey, review the resuscitation management given before arrival to hospital 397

• Search systematically according to ABCDE Trauma Protocol for any signs of major injury such as: - • Head and Eye injury • Dental trauma • Fractured spine • Chest injuries • Internal abdominal/Pelvic injuries • Manage accordingly. Emergency/casualty room set up is mandatory. • Refer if specialist intervention is required Table18.1:A, B, C, D, E Trauma Protocol Assess Intervention A (airway) Is it patent? Place an oral airway. Important investigations for trauma patient - guided by clinical presentation and clinician assessment • Trauma series X-rays (limb x-ray +/- cervical spine, chest, pelvic) • FBP, blood typing, and cross-matching • Random blood glucose • Serum electrolyte • Brain CT scan • Non-contrast CT of fracture ideal for assessing intra articular and complex fractures • MRI to ascertain spinal cord injury and soft tissue injuries or occult fractures • Compartment pressure testing – to rule-out or confirm compartment syndrome • Focused abdominal sonography for trauma (FAST) which is a rapid and focused bedside examination to identify the presence of intraperitoneal or pericardial free fluid 18.2 Timing and Algorithms of Polytrauma Management. Polytrauma patient is the one with two or more significant injuries, includes life threatening injury (severe head, chest, or abdomen injury). The priorities in trauma care are treating the lethal injury first, then reassess and treat again having the following objectives: • life salvage • limb salvage • salvage of total function if possible The steps in management are: • Primary survey • Resuscitation • Secondary survey • Definitive care Figure18.1 Algorithm in managing a polytrauma patient Massive transfusion protocol Massive transfusion protocols are activated by a clinician in response to massive bleeding. Generally, this is activated after transf

ithm in managing a polytrauma patient Massive transfusion protocol Massive transfusion protocols are activated by a clinician in response to massive bleeding. Generally, this is activated after transfusion of 4-10units. MTPs have a predefined ratio of RBCs, FFP/cryoprecipitate, and platelets units (random donor platelets) in each pack (e.g., 1:1:1 or 2:1:1 ratio) for transfusion. 399

18.3 Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI) TBI is defined as an alteration in brain function, or other evidence of brain pathology, caused by an external force. TBI is a leading cause of death and permanent disability worldwide. Primary brain injury occurs at time of trauma (e.g., cortical contusions, brain lacerations, diffuse axonal injury) while secondary injury develops following the initial insult (brain edema, hypoxemia, ischemia due to elevated intracranial pressure). TBI is often associated with ophthalmic, ENT, dental and maxillofacial injuries. Clinical presentat ion • History and/or clinical evidence of blunt or penetrating trauma to the head • Transient or sustained alteration in level of consciousness • Other evidence of altered brain function i.e., seizures, amnesia, focal deficits Clinical classification of TBI • Perform the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) to assess clinical severity of TBI across care paths. • Report each component of GCS; Eye, Verbal, and Motor response, e.g., E4V4M5, not just the total score. 400

• If a GCS component is untestable due to intubation, sedation, etc., record the reason. Table 18.2: Glasgow Coma Scale EYE RESPONSE MOTOR RESPONSE VERBAL RESPONSE Spontaneous--open with Obeys commands for movement Oriented 5points blinking at baseline 4points 6points To verbal stimuli, command, Purposeful movement to painful stimulus Confused conversation, speech 3points 5points but able to answer questions 4points To pain only (not applied to Withdraws in response to pain 4points Inappropriate words face) 2points 3points No response 1point Flexion in response to pain (decorticate Incomprehensible posturing) 3points speech 2points Extension response in response to pain No response 1point (decerebrate posturing) 2points No response 1point Table18.3: Traumatic Brain Injury Severity Classification Based on GCS Mild Traumatic Brain injury • Glasgow coma scale 13–14 • Good prognosis with minimal or no long-term sequel Moderate Traumatic Brain • Glasgow coma scale 9–12 Injury • Fair prognosis Severe Traumatic Brain injury • Glasgow coma scale <8 (comatose) • Poor prognosis with high mortality and long-term disability Management of TBI Prehospital and Lower-Level Health facilities Interventions • Take important history from patient and relatives (AMPLE) • Apply the BLS trauma protocol and perform a rapid neurological assessment • Protect the C-spine stabilize the neck with rigid neck

entions • Take important history from patient and relatives (AMPLE) • Apply the BLS trauma protocol and perform a rapid neurological assessment • Protect the C-spine stabilize the neck with rigid neck collar/improvised alternative devices • Give oxygen therapy to all patients with O saturation <92% (rate of 4L/min) 2 • Give IV normal saline infusion through large gauge cannula • Avoid SBP of <90mmHg in adults and older children • Catheterize the bladder and ensure urine output above 30ml per hour • Refer all polytrauma or isolated TBI patients with GCS<13, to center capable to provide definitive care. • Referred patients should be accompanied by a competent adult during transport. • Inform the destination hospital (by phone or referral letter in non-emergencies) of the impending transfer. Note Do not give dextrose infusion for resuscitation in TBI Referral hospital and higher-level Facilities Interventions • Follow the ATLS ABCD trauma protocol • Send for rapid non-contrast brain CT scan all moderate and severe TBI patients • Consider CT scan in mild TBI cases with following clinical stigmata o signs of basal skull fracture (hemotympanum, racoon eyes, CSF leak, Battle's sign) o post-traumatic seizures or presence of focal neurological deficits, o age 65years or older, any history of bleeding or clotting disorders, o dangerous mechanism of injury or deterioration of GCS Manageme

t-traumatic seizures or presence of focal neurological deficits, o age 65years or older, any history of bleeding or clotting disorders, o dangerous mechanism of injury or deterioration of GCS Management of mild TBI patients • Admit and observe for 24hours all patient with mild TBI and normal CT scan • Discharge with instructions on danger signs that may require hospital readmission. o Seizures, severe headache, focal neurological deficits o Alteration in level of consciousness and confusion • Perform clinical follow up assessment after 2weeks. 401

• Individualize management of mild TBI with abnormal CT findings based on clinical judgement. Management of Moderate and Severe TBI • Multidisciplinary approach is of paramount importance in TBI management. • Admit all moderate and severe TBI patients to ICU for neurocritical care management. • Initiate a Cerebral Perfusion Pressure/Intracranial Pressure (CPP-ICP) oriented protocol. Critical Care Management of Moderate and Severe TBI Management of Airway and Mechanical Ventilation • Consider endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation for all severe TBI patients. • For intubated patients: maintain ETCO2 between 35-45mmHg. (based on ABGs and/capnogram) • Maintain ventilation rates as follows: children, 20breaths/min; adolescents/adults, 10 breaths/min. • Target tidal volume of 6 -7ml/kg to maintain the ETCO2 within the above target range. • Avoid prophylactic hyperventilation except only for obvious signs of herniation. • In such cases, only mild hyperventilation is recommended with ETCO2 target range of 30- 35mmHg. • Consider early tracheostomy to reduce mechanical ventilation days in anticipated prolonged intubation Management of Blood Pressure • Maintain SBP at 100mmHg to ensure cerebral perfusion. • Give IV fluid resuscitation with an initial bolus of 1L of NS and the 20ml/kg. • Consider vasopressors if adequate volume resuscitation does not maintain target MAP Note T

ensure cerebral perfusion. • Give IV fluid resuscitation with an initial bolus of 1L of NS and the 20ml/kg. • Consider vasopressors if adequate volume resuscitation does not maintain target MAP Note Treatment of hypertension: In TBI, the treatment of acute hypertension is not recommended. Restrict IV fluids to a minimal rate in patients with SBP 140mmHg. ICP Monitoring and Management • Monitor ICP using external ventricular drain (EVD) technique (gold standard of ICP monitoring ) • Consider ICP monitoring in GCS 8 or evidence of structural brain damage on initial CT scan. • Initiate ICP treatment to monitored patient whenever ICP increases to 20-25 mm Hg • Perform interval plain CT scan to monitor ICP where no invasive facilities to monitor ICP is available. • Use a “stepladder approach” with escalating treatment measures to manage ICP. o Head of bed elevation at 200 adequate analgesia sedation using short-acting agents o Prescribe hyperosmolar agents in presence of intracranial mass lesions. C: mannitol (IV) 0.25 - 1gm/kg boluses 4-6hourly for 24-72hours. OR C: 3%Sodium chloride (IV) at 3mls/kg 4-6hourly infused over 30min for 24-72hours. • Take precaution in giving Mannitol to hypovolemic patients., prefer Hypertonic saline • Monitor serum sodium levels and osmolality every 6hours during hyperosmolar therapy • Stop if serum osmolality exceeds 320mOsm/L or serum Sodium level e

povolemic patients., prefer Hypertonic saline • Monitor serum sodium levels and osmolality every 6hours during hyperosmolar therapy • Stop if serum osmolality exceeds 320mOsm/L or serum Sodium level exceeds 160mEq/L • Consider barbiturate coma for patients who fail to respond to aggressive measures to control ICP Note The use of steroids is NOT recommend edfor improving outcome or reducing ICP in T BI 402

Neurosurgical Interventions for TBI • Refer for emergency neurosurgical evaluation patient all patients with epidural hematomas (EDH), subdural hematomas (SDH), cerebral contusions, or intracerebral hematomas (ICH) with significant mass effect to the brain. • Consider evacuation of large traumatic hematomas before neurological deterioration develops from hematoma expansion even if a patient has a relatively high GCS score. • A lower threshold for surgical intervention is recommended for posterior fossa mass lesions. • A formal craniotomy is necessary to perform adequate evacuation of intracranial mass lesions • Consider decompressive craniectomy to relieve ICP only when benefits outweigh the risks for such a procedure. • Elevate depressed skull fractures where surgical approach is indicated o Open depressed fractures, gross contamination to prevent infection, o Low bleeding risk from e.g., no risk of dural venous sinus lacerations. Note Depressed skull fracture over the sagittal sinus SHOULD NOT be treated surgically because of the high risk of uncontrollable hemorrhage Extracranial Surgical Interventions • Delay non emergent extracranial surgical interventions in TBI patients unless they are life- saving procedures. • Orthopedic operations should ideally be delayed 24-48 hours to allow initial stabilization of ICP • Consider damage control orthopedics with initial external fix

ss they are life- saving procedures. • Orthopedic operations should ideally be delayed 24-48 hours to allow initial stabilization of ICP • Consider damage control orthopedics with initial external fixation and later definitive treatment to minimize the risk for “second hit” neurological phenomenon. Management of Seizures Related to TBI • Prescribe anticonvulsant to treat overt seizures in all patients presenting with history of seizures • Prescribe prophylactic anticonvulsants in high-risk individuals o severe TBI, mild and moderate TBI with brain contusions, o intracranial hemorrhages, penetrating brain injuries C: phenytoin (IV) 15mg/kg loading dose given in 250mls of 0.9%sodium chloride for 30min, then maintenance dose (PO)100mg 8hourly for 2weeks. OR A: carbamazepine (PO) 200mg 12hourly for 2weeks OR S: levetiracetam (IV/PO) 500mg 12hourly (maximum 1500mg/day) for 2weeks Sedation and Analgesia in TBI D: propofol (IV) 5mcg/kg/min for 3-5min, (increase by 5mcg/kg/min over 5-10min) OR S: fentanyl (IV) 100mcg/2ml bolus stat before desired procedure. Temperature management: maintain normothermia (temperature should be kept <38°C.) D: paracetamol (IV) at 15mg/kg 8hourly for 5 -7days or when required Management of blood sugar • Maintain blood glucose between 4–8 mmol/L, avoid glucose containing fluids unless in treatment of hypoglycemia Nutrition in severe TBI • Initiate enteral f

or when required Management of blood sugar • Maintain blood glucose between 4–8 mmol/L, avoid glucose containing fluids unless in treatment of hypoglycemia Nutrition in severe TBI • Initiate enteral feeding (PO/NGT/OGT) within 24-48hours of injury to attain basal caloric replacement in TBI. • Aim to attain 100% (in paralyzed) and 140% (in non-paralyzed) of the patients resting metabolic expenditure 403

• Consider use of prokinetic agents in TBI patients with gastric feeding intolerance C: metoclopramide (IV) 10mg 8hourly for 5-7days Antibiotic management in TBI • Prescribe antibiotics in penetrating TBI and closed TBI with invasive monitoring and therapeutic treatments, D: ceftriaxone + sulbactam (IV) (FDC) 1.5g 12hourly for 7–14days. OR B: metronidazole (IV) 500mg 8hourly for 7-14days Electrolyte abnormalities in TBI • Perform frequent monitoring of the serum electrolytes and osmolality with aim to maintain within normal ranges. • Specific attention to sodium levels is crucial in TBI patients, as hyponatremia may worsen cerebral edema. • Diabetes insipidus (DI) or the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone (SIADH) are common in TBI Peptic Ulcer Prophylaxis in TBI: C: pantoprazole (IV) 40mg 24hourly for 2weeks Other considerations in TBI • Provide DVT prophylaxis to prevent thromboembolic events. Options for prevention include mechanical • Consider nonpharmacological methods (compression stockings or intermittent pneumatic compression) • Prescribe pharmacological (low-molecular-weight heparin) only in low hemorrhage risk patients • For post neurosurgical intervention, initiate LMWH 72hours postoperative if no contraindications. S: low molecular weight heparin (SC) 40mg 24hourly for 3days while monitoring INR. Management of anemia post TBI: • Consider PRBC or whole blo

tiate LMWH 72hours postoperative if no contraindications. S: low molecular weight heparin (SC) 40mg 24hourly for 3days while monitoring INR. Management of anemia post TBI: • Consider PRBC or whole blood transfusion in TBI patients with hemoglobin <7g/dl. Rehabilitation post TBI • Offer rehabilitation services in TBI patients to improve patient’s ability to return to premorbid function • Involve multidisciplinary specialties to treat resultant mental and physical problems caused by TBI, provide social and emotional support, and assist patients to adapt to changes as occurring during the recovery period. • Consider provision of available both inpatient hospital rehabilitation and outpatient home- based rehabilitation care • Individualize rehabilitation program as tolerated by the respective patients. 18.4 Spinal Cord Injury (SCI) Acute spinal cord injury (SCI) refers to disturbances to patients normal sensory, motor, or autonomic function resulting from trauma. Following the initial insult, up to 25% of spinal cord injuries is thought to occur not at the time of the initial trauma but later during treatment or transport. SCI management, therefore, should be multidisciplinary incorporating appropriate prehospital services, Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) protocols, prompt neurosurgical interventions, and timely rehabilitation. Clinical presentation • Pain • Motor deficit • Num

ating appropriate prehospital services, Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) protocols, prompt neurosurgical interventions, and timely rehabilitation. Clinical presentation • Pain • Motor deficit • Numbness • Pyspnea • Paresthesias Investigations • Spine X-rays • Spine CT scan--assist in decision making and surgical planning. 404

• Spine MRI—decision making and prediction of neurologic outcome. • FAST/eFAST for associated injuries Prehospital and Lower-level health facilities management of SCI • Provide basic life support (BLS) care, and prevent further neurological deficits • Immobilize the cervical spine using rigid neck collar or improvised alternative devices • Advocate spinal immobilization for all patients with suspected or confirmed spine trauma. • Catheterize the bladder if urine retention • Consider immediate transfer to the hospital that can provide specialized spine care Referral level and specialized facilities management of SCI • Follow the Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) protocol evaluation o Assess for intubation-If needed, do Rapid Sequence Intubation (RSI) o Ensure minimal cervical spine manipulation during intubation o Give sedation (if intubated): S: fentanyl (IV) 50 g/hour, continuous for 24-72hours. o Circulation goals - avoid hypotension and bradycardia. o MAP goal 85mmHg for blunt & incomplete penetrating SCI injury o For hypotensive patients: give fluid challenge with a maximum 2 L NS bolus o Persistent hypotension even after adequate fluid resuscitation: start on S: noradrenaline 0.05 g/kg/min titrated to attain MAP goal 85mmHg • Treat shock as per the guideline (refer to emergency care section) • Remove backboard as soon as possible; transfer onto a firm, padded surface/mat

e 0.05 g/kg/min titrated to attain MAP goal 85mmHg • Treat shock as per the guideline (refer to emergency care section) • Remove backboard as soon as possible; transfer onto a firm, padded surface/mattress • Identify and treat life threatening associated injuries. e.g., chest, abdominal visceral trauma • Complete neurological assessment and detailed physical exam • Utilize validated scores e.g., ASIA (refer table 18.4 ) impairment scale to document neurological deficits • Maintain spine alignment all the time during assessment and nursing care—log rolling • Provide pain management (non-intubated patient): S: fentanyl (IV) 25-50 g hourly for 24-72hours then when required OR C: morphine (IV) 2-5mg hourly for 24-72 hours then when required Table 18.4: ASIA Impairment Scale CLASS DESCRIPTION A Complete: No sensory or motor function is preserved in sacral segments S4-S5 B Incomplete: Sensory, but not motor, function is preserved below the neurologic level and extends through sacral segments S4-S5 C Incomplete: Motor function is preserved below the neurologic level, and most key muscles below the neurologic level have a muscle grade of less than 3 D Incomplete: Motor function is preserved below the neurologic level, and most key muscles below the neurologic level have a muscle grade that is greater than or equal to 3 E Normal: Sensory and motor functions are normal Conservative Manag

• Select appropriate surgical intervention and implants based on expert judgment and available resources. • Inform patient and caretakers on the prognosis and expected outcome of all offered interventions Other considerations in SCI care • Steroid use in acute spinal cord injury is still controversial, conform to institutional guidelines • Consider DVT prophylaxis, starting within the first 72hours following trauma S: low molecular heparin (SC) 40mg 24hourly for 3days while monitoring INR. Type and timing of rehabilitation • Provide rehabilitation services to patients with acute SCI as soon as they are medically stable. • Consider ambulation training, conventional overground walking, functional electrical therapy. • Offer assistive ambulation devices e.g., high back wheelchair, axillary crutches. • Give stress ulcer prophylaxis to all patients: C: pantoprazole (IV) 40mg 24hourly for 7-14days Management of Pain and Spasticity in SCIpatients • Score pain using Visual analogue scale (VAS) to assist in clinical decisions • Manage generalized pain with A: diclofenac (IM) 75mg 8hourly for 3-5days OR B: tramadol (PO) 50mg 8hourly for 3-7days. • Manage neuropathic pain with D: pregabalin (PO) 75mg 12hourly for 2weeks • Manage spasticity with S: baclofen (PO) 10mg 8hourly for 2weeks OR S: tizanidine (PO) 2mg 8hourly for 2weeks • Monitor response to therapy and adjust dosages as needed • De-escalate patients from opioids to lowest possible doses as soon as possible Nutrition goals—Maintain or improve nutritional status and minimize weight loss • Provide enteral support within 48hours via NGT/PEG feeding access • Perform bedside glucose checks, maintain euglycemia (blood glucose <180mg/dl) Bladder goals—Prevent autonomic dysreflexia and catheter associated UTI (CAUTI) • Consider removing indwelling urinary catheter when no longer on IV fluids • Begin routine straight catheterization protocol every 4-6 hrs. • Assess patient’s readiness to learn self-intermittent catheterization (SIC) Prevention of Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) • Provide compression devices to bilateral extremities while in bed • Prescribe pharmacological prophylaxis if not contraindicated by other concerns. revention of Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) • Provide compression devices to bilateral extremities while in bed • Prescribe pharmacological prophylaxis if not contraindicated by other concerns. Skin care management • Prevent pressure sores using interval manual repositioning and skin moisturizers • Utilize pressure redistribution devices e.g., air mattresses, sacral silicon dressings • Consider removing collars soon as per neurosurgeon recommendation • Consult relevant wound care specialties as soon as pressure sores are detected Rehabilitation and mobilization plans • Focus to increase functional abilities and prevent contractures • Initiate early out-of-bed to high-back wheelchair every 24hours or as patient tolerates • Ensure pressure relief protocol when patient in wheelchair • Provide chest physiotherapy with patient sitting on edge of bed • Involve Physiotherapy/Orthotics (PT/OT) team to assess need for orthotics • Participate in family counselling meetings to involve their involvement Psychosocial goals for SCI patients • Foster effective copping strategies and provide education to patient and caretakers. 18.5 Chest Trauma Injury to the chest/thorax are generally divided into blunt and penetrating can involve multiple areas of the chest/thorax including, 407

18.6 Abdominal Trauma Unrecognized abdominal injury remains a frequent cause of preventable death after trauma. The initial evaluation in abdominal trauma patient must include the primary and secondary survey There are two basic categories of abdominal trauma: • Penetrating trauma where surgical consultation is important e.g. Gunshot, stabbing. • Non-penetrating trauma e.g., compression, crush, seat belt, acceleration/deceleration injuries. Investigations • FAST/eFAST • Pelvic X-ray • FBP • Diagnostic peritoneal lavage. • Blood grouping and x-match • Abdominal CT scan for stable • CXR patient Management • Goal of acute management is to stabilize the patient and to determine need for urgent laparotomy • An exploratory laparotomy may be the best definitive procedure if abdominal injury needs to be excluded Absolute indications for surgery • Hemodynamic instability • CXR with free air • Peritonitis or drainage of fecal • Evisceration of bowels contents Pharmacological management D: paracetamol (IV) 1g 8hourly for 24hours OR C: morphine (IV) 10mg 6hourly for 24hours 18.6.1 Soft Tissue Injuries Diagnostic criteria • Pain only, traumatic swelling, bruises with intact skin, cuts, abrasions, puncture wounds or open wounds of varying size and severity • Injury to internal organs must be recognized and referred, including subtle signs of organ damage, e.g.: o blood in the urine – kidney

s, puncture wounds or open wounds of varying size and severity • Injury to internal organs must be recognized and referred, including subtle signs of organ damage, e.g.: o blood in the urine – kidney or bladder damage o shock – internal bleeding o blood or serous drainage from the ear or nose – skull base fracture • An injury causing a sprain or strain may be initially overlooked. Exclude fractures by performing appropriate X-rays Note • Closed injuries and fractures of long bones may be serious and damage blood vessels • Contamination with dirt and soil complicates the outcome of treatment Emergency management • Immobilize injured limb after reduction by POP cast or splint • Monitor vital signs • Monitor the arterial pulse and capillary refill below an injury on the limb with swelling. Wound care • Surgical debridement of the wound • Suture or splint when needed • Avoid primary suture if the wound is infected: o Dirty or contaminated o Crushed o In need of debridement 409

Do X- • Deformity ray to exclude bone fractures or • Joint tenderness on bone joint dislocation. • No response to treatment • Progressive swelling • Severe limitation of movement • Extensive bruising 18.7 Fractures of Long Bones and Dislocation Fractures of long bones of upper and lower limbs are quite common. If not properly treated they often lead to long-term deformities. Osteomyelitis is always the complication of open fractures. Hemorrhagic shock may occur in situations involving multiple fractures or pelvic ring fractures. 18.7.1 Fracture A disruption or break in the continuity of the structure of bone caused by direct or indirect vector forces. e.g., compression, traction, and shear). Fractures can be open or closed with different fracture pattern; transverse, oblique, spiral or comminuted. Simple fractures can be treated at the receiving hospital, whereas complex fractures require transfer to specialized hospitals. 18.7.2 Dislocations A dislocation is complete and persistent separation of two or more bones that form a joint with part of the supporting joint capsule and some of its ligaments are disrupted. Dislocations can be congenital or acquired. In subluxation, the bones in a joint are partly out of position. Traumatic dislocations are orthopedic emergency. Clinical presentation • History of Trauma/assault • Swelling, tenderness

or acquired. In subluxation, the bones in a joint are partly out of position. Traumatic dislocations are orthopedic emergency. Clinical presentation • History of Trauma/assault • Swelling, tenderness, misalignment, inability to move or use an affected limb or joint, acute pain caused by movement, open wounds. Investigations • X-Ray limb +/- cervical spine, chest, pelvic • CBC, blood typing, and cross-matching • Non-contrast CT of fracture ideal for assessing intra articular and complex fractures • MRI to ascertain soft tissue injuries or occult fractures • Compartment pressure testing • Abdominal pelvic ultrasound as part of multiple trauma assessment plan. Management Start with A B C D E Trauma Protocol Non-Pharmacological Treatment at Community / dispensary level / health Centre level • Immobilize injured limb by POP cast or splint. • Monitor vital signs. • Monitor the arterial pulse and capillary refill below an injury on the limb with swelling • Refer the patient if open fracture or if specialized services are needed Definitive treatment at Health Centre to hospital level Non-Operative treatment Undisplaced fractures are treated by casting or splint, displaced /angulated factures require closed reduction, followed by a period of immobilization using either, casting or traction (skin and skeletal traction). Traction or cast may be used as part of 1st aid or definitive treat

res require closed reduction, followed by a period of immobilization using either, casting or traction (skin and skeletal traction). Traction or cast may be used as part of 1st aid or definitive treatment. Note Principal for close reduction: Achieve adequate anesthesia (hematoma block/regional/general anesthesia) /analgesia/relaxation. Apply traction to the long axis of the injured limb, reverse the mechanism of injury/fracture. Correct medial/lateral displacement followed by correction of posterior/anterior displacement. Immobilize fracture with cast/splint to maintain reduction to allow the fracture to heal and, at the same time provide the patient with functional aftercare. 411

Hemorrhage is the leading cause of death overall. Associated injuries • Chest injury • Urological injuries • Long bone fractures • Head and abdominal injuries • Spine fractures Classified depending on the direction of force causing the injury • Anterior-posterior compression • Lateral compression • Vertical shear Clinical Presentation • Pain • Laceration of perineum • Inability to bear weight. • Degloving injuries • Limb leg discrepancy with possible • Scrotal, labial,or perineal rotation of one or both extremities. hem

resentation • Pain • Laceration of perineum • Inability to bear weight. • Degloving injuries • Limb leg discrepancy with possible • Scrotal, labial,or perineal rotation of one or both extremities. hematoma • Flank hematoma Investigations • FBP, grouping Cross-matching • chemistry panel Imaging Studies • Radiography o AP, and JUDET VIEWS and trauma series radiography whenever necessary (Cervical, Chest and Pelvis) o Abdominal pelvic USS o CT (contrast enhanced) o Pelvic angiography o Cystography o MRI o Retrograde urethrogram Management Start with ABCDE Trauma Protocol irrespective of the level of facility Non-Pharmacological Treatment at Community / dispensary level / health Centre level • Immobilize pelvic fracture with Pelvic binder/sheet • Monitor vital signs. • Monitor the arterial pulse and capillary refill below an injury on the limb with swelling • Refer patients with pelvic fractures upon resuscitation. Can be extension type (95%) or flexion type (5%). Further classified by Gartland as type I, II and III in order of severity of injury. Clinical presentation History of injury, swelling, subcutaneous ecchymosis, pain, limited range of motion, bony crepitus, deformity, tenderness around the elbow, may include loss of sensation and loss of pulse to the forearm if accompanied with nerve or vascular injury. Investigation • Plain x-ray (AP & L) • CT scan for complex fractures • Doppler ultrasound if suspecting • CT angiography for complex vascular injury fracture with vascular injury. Management Follow trauma management protocol for prehospital, primary facility and hospital Table 18.10 Definitiv

suspecting • CT angiography for complex vascular injury fracture with vascular injury. Management Follow trauma management protocol for prehospital, primary facility and hospital Table 18.10 Definitive management Type Management Gartland’s Type-I and Type-IIA Closed manipulation and reduction under general anesthesia or deep sedation. Maintenance of reduction with a plaster slab or arm cast and sling for three weeks. Confirm reduction by X-ray. Start physiotherapy after three weeks Gartland’s Type-IIB and Type-III Close reduction under image intensifier and percutaneous pinning with Kirschner. Can be acute or chronic. Clinical presentation • History of Trauma or repeated extensive exercises • Severe pain at rest or with any movement especially passive stretching of the muscles. Pain out of proportion to original injury should raise suspicion. •

y of Trauma or repeated extensive exercises • Severe pain at rest or with any movement especially passive stretching of the muscles. Pain out of proportion to original injury should raise suspicion. • Paresthesia • Late stage Pallor, Pulselessness and Poikilothermia 416

Investigation • Urine myoglobin • Complete Blood Count • Urinalysis • Coagulation studies • X-ray • Creatine phosphokinase (CPK) • Ultra sound, • Serum creatinine • CT, • Serum electrolyte • MRI • Measure compartment pressure (Using Tonometer/ fiberoptic transducer, /Simple needle/, Wick Catheter/, Slit catheter) Non-pharmacological management • Bandages and casts must be removed. • Do not elevate the affected limb above the level of the heart • Elevation will impede perfusion by decreasing arterial pressure • Provide nutritional support • Provide early rehabilitation to minimize complications Pharmacological Management Hydration • Facilitate renal clearance of myoglobin (aim urine output at 1-2 mL/kg/hr) • Infuse intravenous fluids at 500 mL/hr. 0.9% sodium chloride or compound sodium lactate crystalloid solution • Consider 22.4mEq sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine • Hemoglobin and myoglobin are more soluble in an alkaline solution. 0mg 8hourly for 24-72hours • Change to oral medication as soon as a patient can tolerate Prophylaxis medication • Antivenoms for snake or insect bite and tetanus immunoglobulin and toxoid in case of animal bite or penetrating trauma Surgical – fasciotomy • Compartment pressures >30mmHg, is an indication for fasciotomy • Perform single or two incision are made to release intra-compartmental pressure depending on the site • Wounds are often closed by skin grafting. Note Acute compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency which requires surgical management within 6-8hours, patient diagnosed in primary health care facilities should be referred immediately. The referring professional should inform the destination hospital (by phone) of the impending transfer. 417

urns Assessment for Inhalation Injury • Inhalation injury usually appears within 2~48 hours post burn due to inhalation of combustion products. • Symptoms: rales, rhonchi, stridor, cough and laboured or rapid breathing. • Use high flow humidified oxygen to prevent drying of mucosa and hypoxia • Airway assessment for inhalation injury includes: o Respiratory distress or upper o Burns around face or neck airway obstruction o Oedema of face or lips 418

o Oropharyngeal carbon or o Impaired consciousness carbonaceous sputum o These signs indicate the need o Singed nasal hairs, eyebrows, to intubate eyelashes o It is better to intubate early, o Inflammation or oedema of than wait for respiratory oropharynx distress. Fluid Resuscitation Principles • F ormulas for fluids budgets and need adjustment on physiological response • Popular formulas: Modified Brooke (2ml) and Parkland (4ml)/kg/%BSA) • Maintenance fluid is required in addition to resuscitation fluid for children • Indicators of response: o Urine output (1ml/kg/hr in children and 0.5 ml/kg/hr in adult) o Normal mentation, perfused extremities o Mixed venous O 2 >70%, normal arterial pH and lactate • Colloid (Albumin, Dextran 70) in the 2nd 24 hours (0.3-0.5ml/kg/BSA) • Add 5%Dextrose as necessary (2L in adult) in the 2nd 24hours to maintain urinary output ii. 0.9% sodium chloride, compound sodium lactate) Pharmacological management A: omeprazole (PO) 20mg 24hourly for 2weeks OR C: pantoprazole (PO) 40mg 24hourly for 2weeks. OR S: esomeprazole (PO) 20mg 24hourly for 2weeks Specific Treatment: Burn Wound • The aim is to cover the wound, reduce infection and allow healing and epithelialization • Unburned areas should be covered to avoid hypothermia, especially in a child. Non-pharmacological management • Local wound care Pharmacological management Topical Agents A: silver sulphadiazine 0.5% apply 24hourly for 14days OR C: silver nitrate pencil 0.5% apply 24hourly for 14days OR A: povidone iodine 10% apply 24hourly for 14days Surgical management. • Early Excision and Grafting • Escharotomy at 2-7days Nutrition • Metabolic needs proportional to BSA up to 50% • Caloric needs calculated by the Harris Benedict equation and may be enteral/parenteral 419

Physical and occupational therapy • rehabilitation to avoid contractures Burn Complications i. Invasive burn wound sepsis • Diagnosis based on examination and histopathology • Possible Organisms: S .aureus, Pseudomonas, E. coli and Proteus spp. Surgical treatment • Debridement • Drainage Pharmacological management B: ceftriaxone (IV) 1-2g 24hourly for 7-10days AND B: metronidazole (IV) 500mg 8hourly for 7-10days Depending on culture results give, S: piperacillin-tazobactam (IV) 4.5gm 6-8hourly 7-10days OR S: meropenem 1g (IV) 8hourly 7-10days. ii. Bacteraemia and Septicaemia • Administer antibiotics based on laboratory diagnosis of infection v. : pantoprazole (IV) 40mg 24hourly for 7-14days vi. Cutaneous complications • Contractures and burn scar Contractures in burn can occur anywhere, its more common wherein flexibility and mobility is present like along the joint, eyelids, cheeks lips neck, elbow and knee. Contractures can be intrinsic by loss of tissue or extrinsic by pull during healing phase contraction. • Treatment of contractures o Release of contracture surgically and use of full thickness or split thickness skin graft or Z /W plasty or different flaps • Supportive treatment post-surgery o Physiotherapy and rehabilitation o Pressure garment to prevent hypertrophic scars o Antihistamine and moisturizing cream for management of itching. 420

vii. Hypertrophic scar and Keloid • Hypertrophic scars--widened or unsightly scar that does not extend beyond the original boundaries of the wound • Keloids--enlarged, raised itching scar possibly painful, extending over normal tissues often resulting from trauma or surgical intervention. Keloid scar can be pink, red, skin –coloured or darker than the surrounding skin Non-surgical Treatment Treatment modality Hypertrophic Keloid Serial intralesional steroid injection 40mg/ml repeat every 3 weeks Manual massage Silicon gel sheeting Pressure garments/compression dressings worn 23 out of 24 hours/day Cryotherapy – cryoprobe liquid nitrogen delivered by cryoprobe Laser -585nm pdl Radiation – used as an adjunct to prevent recurrence Surgical treatment Simple excision, providing wound closure without undue tension 421

CHAPTER NINETEEN METABOLIC AND ENDOCRINE DISEASE CONDITIONS 19.1 Diabetes Mellitus Diabetes mellitus is a clinical syndrome characterized by persistently high blood glucose values due to deficiency or diminished effectiveness of insulin. Classification Diabetes mellitus can be classified as follows: • Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) – due to autoimmune β-cell destruction • Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) – due to a progressive loss of β-cell insulin secretion frequently with underlying insulin resistance • Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) – diabetes diagnosed in the second or third trimester of pregnancy that was not clearly overt diabetes prior to gestation • Specific types of diabetes – due to other causes e.g., monogenic, diseases of the exocrine pancreas and drugs or chemicals. Diagnostic Criteria • Main clinical features of diabetes are thirst, polydipsia, polyuria, tiredness, loss of weight, blurring of vision. • Many people have no classical symptoms and may only present late with the symptoms related to complications e.g., pruritus vulvae and balanitis due to infections, paraesthesia or pain in the limbs, non-healing ulcers, and recurrent bacterial infections. WHO diagnostic Criteria 2006 • Fasting plasma glucose level ≥ 7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL) • Plasma glucose≥ 11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) two hours after a 75 g oral glucose load in a glucose tolerance test • Symptoms of

stic Criteria 2006 • Fasting plasma glucose level ≥ 7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL) • Plasma glucose≥ 11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) two hours after a 75 g oral glucose load in a glucose tolerance test • Symptoms of hypogycaemia and casual plasma glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) • Glycatedhemoglobin (HbA1c) ≥ 6.5%. Diagnosis of gestational diabetes (WHO criteria 2013) • Fasting plasma glucose 5.1–6.9 mmol/l • 2–hour plasma glucose 8.5–11.0 mmol/l following a 75g oral glucose load. Note Fasting plasma glucose higher than 6.9 mmol/l or 2–hour plasma glucose higher than 11.0 mmol/l are considered overt diabetes rather than GDM At risk screening Early diagnosis and good control reduce the risk of costly complications and reduces the deterioration of islet function in T2DM. The following people should therefore be screened with fasting blood glucose or HbA1c at least yearly when they visit health facilities: • Those aged ≥40 years • Children and adults <40 years who are overweight or obese and who have two or more additional risk factors for diabetes • Individuals with impaired glucose tolerance or impaired fasting glucose or a history of a cardiovascular event • People on long-term steroids or immunosuppressants • Women with a history of gestational diabetes mellitus or polycystic ovary syndrome • All pregnant women at the first antenatal visit if overweight, have had gestational diabetes, babies

uppressants • Women with a history of gestational diabetes mellitus or polycystic ovary syndrome • All pregnant women at the first antenatal visit if overweight, have had gestational diabetes, babies with birth weight >4 kg, previous stillbirths or neonatal deaths. Screening should be repeated in the second trimester if negative. • All women during the 2nd or 3rd trimester (for gestational diabetes). Impaired glucose tolerance For people with glucose intolerance the risk of T2DM and its associated mortality may be reduced by: 422

• dietary modification (see chapter on Nutrition) • physical activity (see chapter on Nutrition) • weight reduction • medication with metformin. Goals of diabetes management Table 19.1: Goals for Optimum Management Of Diabetes Diet Advise same as for people without diabetes Body mass index Therapeutic goal is 5–10% weight loss for people who are overweight or obese, but aim for BMI<25 kg/m2 Waist circumference <102 cm for men, <88 cm for women Physical activity At least 30 minutes of moderate physical activity on most days of the week (total ≥150 minutes/week) Cigarette consumption 0 per day Alcohol consumption Not more than 2 standard unit drinks (20 g) per day for men and 1 unit for women Blood glucose level Non-pregnant: 4–6 mmol/L fasting; 6–8 mmol/L postprandial Pregnant: ≤5.0 mmol/L fasting, ≤6.7 mmol/L postprandial Self-monitoring of blood glucose is essential to improve outcomes HbA1c Target ≤7% (6.5–7.5%). Should be measured every three months until stable, thereafter at least twice a year Lipids Total cholesterol: <5.2 mmol/L HDL-C: ≥1.0 mmol/L LDL-C: <2.6 mmol/L Triglycerides: <1.7 mmol/L Blood pressure Target ≤140/90 mmHg For those with albuminuria/proteinuria <130/80 mmHg Urine albumin Spot collection: <20 mg/L Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR): women: <3.5 mg/mmol; men: <2.5 mg/mmol 423

Majority of people with T2DM are overweight or obese and need to lose weight. o Attain and maintain near normal blood glucose levels to prevent hypo- and hyperglyc

aintenance of desirable body weight. Majority of people with T2DM are overweight or obese and need to lose weight. o Attain and maintain near normal blood glucose levels to prevent hypo- and hyperglycaemia and so minimize the onset of chronic complications. o Attain optimum blood lipids and blood pressure control and so reduce the risk of macrovascular disease. • Appropriate measures 424 No N o s N e o Y Yes N o

o Each meal should consist of a variety of foods from the core food groups (vegetables, fruits, whole grains, meat and proteins, dairy products) o Distribute foods evenly throughout the day with small/light meals in between the three main meals. • Food composition o Protein: 15-20% of total energy (1g/kg/day) o Energy from carbohydrates: 45-60% of total calories ü Less than 100gms carbohydrates per day is not advisable as it leads to ketosis ü The distribution and amount of carbohydrates between meals should be synchronized with the action of insulin and drugs. ü Distribute the food over three meals with snacks in between rather than 1 or 2 large meals ü Give more of carbohydrate as complex starches e.g. whole grain cereals, whole grain bread, roots and stem tubers, because they breakdown more slowly to releaseglucose. ü Avoid simple sugars, sugar-sweetened beverages and honey. o Fats should provide < 30% ofenergy ü A lower fat intake of up to 20% or less of the daily energy in case of obese adult with diabetes. o Limit salt to less than 1 teaspoonful/day o Limit fat intake • Physical activity o Aerobic (e.g. brisk walking) and muscle strengthening activities improve glucose tolerance, energy expenditure, feeling of wellbeing and mood, work capacity, improved blood pressure, lipid profiles and increased functional mobility in older people. o Adjust medicine dosages and or food

nce, energy expenditure, feeling of wellbeing and mood, work capacity, improved blood pressure, lipid profiles and increased functional mobility in older people. o Adjust medicine dosages and or food intake to avoid hypoglycaemia. • No smoking • Avoid or limit alcohol intake to one drink/day for women, two drinks/day for men. Pharmacological Treatment Treatment with oral hypoglycemics • Review the blood glucose at follow-up clinic and adjust medicines as needed until blood glucose is controlled. • If lifestyle measures on their own failure achieve blood glucose control or blood glucose levels are persistently high (fasting >11 mmol/l or random >15 mmol/l) initiate: 67267 A: metformin (PO) 500mg 12hourly with or after meals. Increase, as required, until a maximum of 2000mg in 2–3 divided doses. If Metformin is contraindicated, then use A: glibenclamide (PO) 2.5–10mg 12hourly OR C: glimepiride (PO) 1–4mg 12hourly OR A: gliclazide (PO) 40–160mg 12hourly • If the maximum dose of metformin does not result in adequate glycaemic control, either one of the above sulphonylureas may be added, starting with the lower dose and increasing until control is achieved or the maximum dose is reached. • If a combination of both medicines is still inadequate, then insulin should be added as detailed below in the section on insulin. Note • Activity of sulphonylureas is prolonged in both hepatic and

. • If a combination of both medicines is still inadequate, then insulin should be added as detailed below in the section on insulin. Note • Activity of sulphonylureas is prolonged in both hepatic and renal failure • Metformin is contraindicated in those with severe renal, liver and cardiac failure. • The lower dosage is appropriate when initiating treatment in elderly patients with uncertain meal schedules, or in patients with mild hyperglycemia • Sulphonylureas are best taken 15 to 30 minutes before meals Treatment with Insulin Injection in Type 2 Diabetes Insulin injections are indicated in T2DM in the following conditions: 425

• Initial presentation with fasting blood glucose more than 15 mmol/l • Presentation in hyperglycaemic emergency • Peri-operative period especially major or emergency surgery • Other medical conditions requiring tight glycaemic control • Organ failure: Renal, liver, heart etc, • Diabetes not well controlled with diet or oral drugs • Latent autoimmune diabetes of adults (LADA) • Contraindications to oral drugs Note The maximum glucose lowering efficacy of oral drugs is usually evident by six months. Therefore, the efficacy of any added therapy must be assessed within six months and an alternative drug instituted in case of failure Insulin injections should be initiated by a doctor able to fully instruct the patient in its use but insulin will be available at lower health facilities for management of stable patients who require prescription refills. stitution therapy Oral medicines are discontinued (except metformin if patient is obese) S: Pre-mixed insulin is introduced at a dosage of 0.2 IU/kg body weight and this is split into: ⅔ in the morning and ⅓ in the evening • Inject 30 minutes before the morning and the evening meals for human insulins • Inject 0 to 15minutes before meal for analog insulins. Insulin as supplemental therapy Oral medicines are continued A: neutral protamine hagedorn (isophane) insulin administered at night before 22:00h at a total daily dose of 0.1–0.2 IU/kg body weight. • The oral medicines are continued (half maximum dose of sulphonylureas and metformin dose of 2 g/day) • Blood glucose levels are monitored. Self-monitoring in patients with T2DM Self-Monitoring Blood Glucose (SMBG) is usually recommended in the following patients: • patients on insulin and glucose lowering agents that can cause hypoglycaemia (sulphonylureas) • when monitoring hyperglycaemia arising from illness • with pregnancy and pre-pregnancy planning • when changes in treatment, lifestyle or other conditions requires data on glycaemic patterns • when HbA1c estimations are unreliable (eg haemoglobinopathies) TYPE 1 Diabetes Mellitus (Childhood diabetes) Non-pharmacological Treatment • Nutrition (refer to nutrition chapter) • Exercise requirements are as for children without diabetes • Psychological support is important to cope

us (Childhood diabetes) Non-pharmacological Treatment • Nutrition (refer to nutrition chapter) • Exercise requirements are as for children without diabetes • Psychological support is important to cope with this chronic illness. Pharmacological Treatment Total dose of insulin contains soluble and intermediate/long-acting insulin. 426

• Give total dose of insulin o pre puberty 0.5 IU/kg/day subcutaneously for life o puberty 1-2 IU/kg/day subcutaneously for life o after puberty < 2 IU/kg/day subcutaneously for life • Divide total insulin dose as per recommended percentage below Table 19.3: Recommended Insulin Dosage Time Type of insulin Proportion Break-fast Short acting 30% of total daily dose Lunch Short acting 20% of total daily dose Supper Short acting 10% of total daily dose Bedtime Long/intermediate acting 40% of total daily dose Note • Diabetes in children is often misdiagnosed as some other condition – e.g. as pneumonia or asthma (laboured breathing), • Insulin should be given for life and the dose adjusted according to weight and glucose response • Self-glucose monitoring by glucometer at home 3-4times/day • Pre-mixed insulins not advised in children Clinical monitoring of people with diabetes Table 19.4: Clinical Monitoring of People with Diabetes Initial visit 3 Month visit Annual visit History and diagnosis • Relevant history History and examination Physical examination • Weight –as at initial visit • Height and weight • Blood pressure • Waist/Hip circumference • Foot inspection Biochemistry as at initial visit • Blood pressure • Blood Glucose • Detailed foot examination • Urine protein • Tooth inspection • Education advice • Eye examination • Nutrition advice • Visual acuity+ Fundoscopy • Review

tial visit • Blood pressure • Blood Glucose • Detailed foot examination • Urine protein • Tooth inspection • Education advice • Eye examination • Nutrition advice • Visual acuity+ Fundoscopy • Review therapy • ECG • HbA1c every six • Biochemistry months o Blood sugar o Glycosylated Haemoglobin (HbA1c) o Lipid profile (TC, HDC, LDLC, TG) o Serum creatinine o Urine: glucose, ketones, protein • Education • Nutritional advice • Medication if needed TC=Total cholesterol, HDLC=high density lipoprotein, LDLC= low density lipoprotein, G=Triglycerides Surgery in diabetes General measures Correct pre-operative management depends on type of surgery (major or minor), type of diabetes and recent diabetes control. • Surgery should be delayed if possible if HBA1C >9% or blood glucose fasting >10 mmol/l or random glucose > 13 mmol/l. • Screen for nephropathy, cardiac disease, retinopathy and neuropathy and inform surgical team. If diabetes is well controlled and surgery is minor: • If on diet or oral agent therapy, omit therapy on morning of surgery and resume therapy when eating normally • For T1DM, continue with normal dose of insulin 427

If on insulin therapy or poor glycaemic control or major surgery: • Use continuous IV insulin infusion • Start at 8 am and stop when eating normally. • Monitor blood glucose before, during and after surgery: aim for blood glucose levels of 6–10 mmol/l. For major surgery (glucose-insulin potassium regimen) • Once snack is missed, start an IV regimen irrespective of the size of the procedure • Maintain insulin administration (hourly) to avoid lipolysis and ketoacidosis in patients with restricted oral intake and thus prevent DKA • Administer 5% dextrose in maintenance IV fluids AND • Short-acting insulin (16 Units) + KCl 10mmol/L added to 500mls of 10% dextrose. o Infuse at 80ml/hr IV. o If obese or initial blood glucose is high (>14mmo/l) consider higher dose of insulin (20 Units) o If very thin or usual insulin dose is very low, consider lower dose (12 Units) • Monitor blood glucose levels hourly (aim for 6–10 mmo/l) o If blood glucose is low or falling reduce dose by 4 Units o If blood glucose is high or raising increase dose by 4 Units • Patients receiving Multiple Daily Insulin Therapy (MDIT) should receive preoperative basal insulin dose without interruption in the perioperative period. When oral intake is restricted, regular insulin may be given every 4–6 hrs to control hyperglycemia. When a diet is tolerated, the MDIT regimen should be resumed • Post operatively: give 5–1

e period. When oral intake is restricted, regular insulin may be given every 4–6 hrs to control hyperglycemia. When a diet is tolerated, the MDIT regimen should be resumed • Post operatively: give 5–10% dextrose IV 1 litre + KCl 20ml + 2/3 of total daily dose of insulin over 8hrs and repeat until able to take orally. Continue the infusion until 60 minutes after the first meal. • Resume usual therapy after first meal • Check electrolytes daily. 19.2 Management of Diabetes During Religious Fasting There are several types of fasting: • Normal fast (the common fast): the person abstains from all foods but can take water for a limited time. • Total fast = total abstinence from both food and water. This should not go beyond a maximum of three days and is not recommended for those people taking insulin secretagogues or insulin. • Partial fast = abstain from selected foods and drinks, or omit a certain meal each of the fasting days. Those with very poor glucose control should be discouraged from fasting • A total fast is not recommended for anyone with diabetes. Adequate hydration is important even during the period of fasting. • For those on insulin, a partial fast is preferred to total or normal forms of fasting. • Self-blood glucose monitoring is mandatory for people with diabetes who elect to fast. o Once-a-day is adequate for patients on diet only or diet with metformin. o At leas

r normal forms of fasting. • Self-blood glucose monitoring is mandatory for people with diabetes who elect to fast. o Once-a-day is adequate for patients on diet only or diet with metformin. o At least 3 times a day in patients on insulin secretagogues o More frequently if hyperglycaemia is marked and the urine tested for ketones. • Consider terminating the fast if frequent hypoglycaemia or intercurrent infections. • Vigorous activity should be avoided during period of fast. • Ensure ready access to a healthcare provider during the period of fast. Management of normal fasting for people treated with oral hypoglycaemic agents • Fasting is possible in this situation. • Usual dietary advice should be followed at this time. • Patients on metformin, alpha-glucosidase inhibitors and thiazolidinediones (glitazones) can continue taking the usual doses at the usual times. • If on a second or third generation sulphonylurea (glibenclamide, gliclazide, glipizide, glimepiride), this should be taken at the time of breaking the fast and not before dawn. 428

Management of normal fasting for T2DM patients on insulin • If on once daily insulin before bed, this can be given as usual • If on twice daily short- and intermediate-acting insulin: o Before the dawn meal, give the usual evening dose of short-acting insulin without any intermediate-acting insulin. o Before the evening meal give the usual morning dose of short-acting and intermediate-acting insulin. • If on basal bolus regimen: o Usual doses of the short-acting insulin can be given before the dawn and evening meals, and usual doses of the intermediate-acting insulin can still be given at 10pm. o Regular SBGM is essential to ensure prevention of hypoglycaemia o Titration of doses should occur according to SBGM results. • Neither the insulin injection nor the breaking of the skin for SBGM will break the fast.
